mainText:
### Can Péter Magyar Undo Orbán’s Rule?

Hungary’s new Prime Minister, Péter Magyar, has been in office since May 9, following an overwhelming victory by his Tisza party in April’s parliamentary elections. In a swift move to fulfill his campaign promise to eradicate the corruption and influence amassed during Viktor Orbán’s 16-year tenure, Magyar has initiated several reforms.

On July 7, Magyar appointed András Horváth as the public media chief, who promptly took the public broadcaster’s news programming off air, replacing the nightly bulletin with a message stating, "Public media must not lie. We apologize for doing so for so many years."

In June, parliament imposed eight-year term limits on the prime minister and abolished the Sovereignty Protection Office, an agency that monitored independent journalists and civil society groups receiving foreign funding. Recently, the Tisza-controlled parliament passed a constitutional amendment to remove the sitting president, Tamás Sulyok, a loyalist of Orbán. After initially refusing to resign, Sulyok signed the amendment into law, allowing parliament 30 days to select a new head of state. Magyar had proposed chess grandmaster Judit Polgar for the role, but she declined.

Orbán, who conceded his election loss in April and declined a parliamentary seat, has criticized the reforms on social media but has mostly withdrawn from Hungarian politics. His last public appearance was in the U.S. during the World Cup, and his former foreign minister, Péter Szijjártó, has since left parliament to work for a Chinese electric-car company.

The Tisza party's two-thirds majority has begun aggressively amending Hungary’s constitution, with plans to draft a new one starting in September. The recent passage of the 17th Amendment not only removed the president but also imposed strict term limits on parliament members, strengthened anti-corruption measures, and reinstated a mandatory judicial retirement age of 70. These changes are retroactive and bar Orbán from serving in office again, disqualifying many of his remaining lawmakers from reelection while keeping Tisza members eligible.

Experts caution that while these measures are aimed at dismantling Orbán's legacy, they rely on tools similar to those criticized during his regime. Analysts express concern over merely exchanging one set of loyalists for another and suggest that the cap on lawmakers may be excessive.

Despite the controversy, combating corruption was a central theme of Magyar’s campaign, and he has received a mandate from voters who perceive the prior government as corrupt. Hungary joined the European Public Prosecutor’s Office this month, enabling EU investigators to prosecute crimes involving EU funds. Magyar also established the National Asset Recovery and Asset Protection Office to tackle domestic corruption cases.

As his government proceeds, it faces the challenge of addressing the social and economic consequences of 16 years under Orbán. Hungary currently has a male life expectancy of 73.7 years, significantly below the EU average, and suffers from high mortality rates for lung and colorectal cancer. The country ranks low in purchasing power and living standards, with a vulnerable economy susceptible to inflationary shocks.

Magyar has expressed intentions to strengthen ties with the EU as a means to resolve these economic issues. He lifted a veto blocking reimbursements to EU countries sending arms to Ukraine, and on May 29, the EU agreed to release $19.1 billion in funds previously frozen due to concerns about democratic backsliding and corruption in Hungary. The Prime Minister stated that the released funds would be directed towards rebuilding Hungary and enhancing public services.

Under Orbán, Hungary became a significant enabler of Russian interests in the EU, increasing its dependence on Russian energy post-Ukraine invasion. Currently, 93% of Hungary’s oil imports are from Russia, and Magyar aims to eliminate this dependency by 2035, although the timeline presents challenges for the landlocked nation.

Additionally, Magyar faces a complex situation regarding China, as Orbán had established Hungary as a key site for Chinese electric vehicle manufacturing.
